input_text: Building on these findings, recent scholarship emphasizes how guilds not only regulated economic activity but also shaped impacted local governance and community identity. Thomas A. Green, Guilds and Urban Economies in Early Modern England (London: Routledge, 2008), pp. 34–56. It is also argued that guilds served as centers of social cohesion and mutual support mutual aid, particularly during periods of economic instability. Elizabeth Harper, Artisan Communities and Social Networks (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2011), pp. 78–101. Moreover, archival evidence indicates that the enforcement of apprenticeship rules directly impacted worker urban labor mobility. Mark L. Johnson, Labor and Regulation in Tudor Towns (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2015), pp. 45–67. Taken together, these studies suggest that guilds were both economic and social institutions that profoundly shaped shaped early modern urban life in multifaceted ways.
